title: How an Old Plate Powers Yellowstone's Volcanoes
summary: Yellowstone is a special place in North America with big volcanoes that sometimes erupt and cover the land with ash. Scientists used to think a hot blob from deep inside Earth, called a mantle plume, made these volcanoes. But a new study says something else might be causing them. Long ago, a giant plate called the Farallon plate pushed under North America and helped build the West Coast. Even though most of this plate is gone now, it left behind stresses in the ground. These stresses open up paths for hot, melted rock to travel up from deep below and reach the surface at Yellowstone. This idea explains why Yellowstone has volcanoes even though it's not near the edge of a plate. The study uses a model to show how the rock moves through the crust, with two branches feeding different areas. This helps us understand Earth's history and how volcanoes work.

questions.correct_answer: How rock moves through the crust

background_read: Yellowstone is a national park in the United States with geysers and volcanoes. Earth has layers like the crust and mantle. Plates are big pieces of Earth's crust that move slowly. When plates push together, they can make mountains or volcanoes. The Farallon plate was one such plate that disappeared under North America long ago, and scientists study how it affects things today, like the volcanoes at Yellowstone.
Article_Structure: WHAT: A new idea that an old plate, not a hot blob, powers Yellowstone's volcanoes
WHY: To understand how volcanoes form and Earth's history

perspectives.description: Scientists thought a mantle plume from deep inside Earth made Yellowstone's volcanoes.
The new study suggests stresses from the old Farallon plate open paths for melted rock to reach Yellowstone.
